---
title: "Mixed Methods and Methodological Pluralism"
description: "Learn what mixed-methods research is and its main designs (sequential, concurrent, embedded), and distinguish it from methodological pluralism as a disciplinary stance — two different ways of taking the limits of any single method seriously."
author:
  - "Claudius Gräbner-Radkowitsch"
  - "Anna-Katharina Kothe"
date: 2026-06-17
date-modified: 2026-06-17
image: ../../assets/images/placeholder.png

learning-objectives:
  - "Define mixed-methods research and explain why complex phenomena can call for it"
  - "Distinguish sequential, concurrent and embedded mixed-methods designs"
  - "Differentiate mixed methods (one study) from methodological pluralism (a disciplinary stance)"
  - "Explain how combining methods can reveal aspects of a phenomenon a single method would miss"

categories:
  - qualitative-methods
  - mixed-methods
  - research-design
  - reproducibility
  - BA
  - concept

level:
  - BA
  - MA

content-type: concept

citation:
  type: entry
  container-title: "Methods Hub"
  title: "Mixed Methods and Methodological Pluralism"
  author:
    - name: "Claudius Gräbner-Radkowitsch"
    - name: "Anna-Katharina Kothe"
  editor:
    - name: "Claudius Gräbner-Radkowitsch"
  url: https://euf-methodology-hub.netlify.app/resources/qualitative-methods/mixed-methods-and-methodological-pluralism
---

# Introduction

Once you see that qualitative and quantitative approaches each have characteristic strengths and blind spots (see [Qualitative vs Quantitative Research](qualitative-vs-quantitative-research.qmd)), an obvious move suggests itself: combine them. This page introduces **mixed-methods research** — combining approaches within a single study — and **methodological pluralism** — a broader stance about the diversity of methods a discipline should sustain. They are related but operate at different levels, and confusing them is a common mistake.

# Why Combine Methods?

Two motivations stand out:

- **Both qualitative and quantitative approaches have pitfalls.** Each illuminates some features of a phenomenon and obscures others.
- **Complex phenomena require multiple perspectives.** A single lens, however sharp, captures only one facet.

The cartoon of blindfolded scientists around an elephant — one declaring "it's a rope!" (the tail), another "it's a wall!" (the side), another "it's a spear!" (the tusk) — captures the predicament. Each observer reports accurately and reaches a different conclusion. Combining perspectives is what reveals the elephant.

# Mixed-Methods Research

Mixed methods is, in one standard definition:

> *"a class of research where the researcher mixes or combines quantitative and qualitative research techniques, methods, approaches, or concepts into a single study."*

The key phrase is **single study**: mixed methods answers **one research question** by drawing on both kinds of evidence. Three common designs:

- **Sequential designs** — one method is used first, and its findings inform the second. *(e.g. exploratory interviews surface hypotheses that a later survey tests; or a survey identifies a puzzle that follow-up interviews explain.)*
- **Concurrent designs** — quantitative and qualitative methods are used **simultaneously**, then compared or combined.
- **Embedded designs** — one method is **nested within** the framework of the other (e.g. a small interview component embedded inside a primarily quantitative trial).

A cartoon often used to summarise the field shows three figures: one insisting *"qualitative methods are best"* (constructivism, inductive logic, exploratory research), another *"quantitative methods are best"* (post-positivism, deductive logic, confirmatory research), and a third in the middle holding that *"the research question drives everything"* (pragmatism, abductive logic, combining QUAL and QUANT). The middle position is the spirit of mixed methods.

# Methodological Pluralism

**Methodological pluralism** operates one level up. Where mixed methods combines approaches to answer a *single* research question, methodological pluralism is about employing a **multitude of methods at the level of a whole discipline**.

Key distinctions:

- **Plurality vs. pluralism.** Plurality is the mere fact that many methods exist; pluralism is the considered stance that this diversity is *valuable* and worth cultivating.
- It plays out on the levels of **ontology, epistemology and methodology**.
- There are **different forms of pluralism** — *consensual*, *agonistic*, and *antagonistic* — depending on how the coexisting positions relate to one another.
- Crucially, it **plays out at the disciplinary level, not in regard to a single research question.**

::: {.callout-note}
## The key take-away

Different methodological approaches can reveal different aspects of social phenomena that might be **missed by a single method**. This is true within a study (mixed methods) and across a discipline (pluralism) — but the two work at different scales and should not be conflated.
:::

::: {.callout-note .level-advanced}
## 🎓 MA / PhD

The distinction between *consensual*, *agonistic* and *antagonistic* pluralism is worth unpacking. Consensual pluralism treats rival approaches as complementary pieces of one puzzle; agonistic pluralism keeps them in productive but unresolved tension; antagonistic pluralism holds that some positions are genuinely incompatible and competing. Which form is appropriate is itself contested, and bears on debates about **paradigm incommensurability** (can findings from incompatible paradigms even be combined?) and on the integrity of mixed-methods inference. In economics and management specifically, methodological pluralism is also an argument about the *sociology* of a discipline — about which approaches get funded, published and taught.
:::

# Summary

- **Mixed methods** combines qualitative and quantitative techniques within a **single study** to answer one research question; main designs are **sequential**, **concurrent** and **embedded**.
- **Methodological pluralism** is a **disciplinary-level** stance valuing a diversity of methods; it distinguishes plurality from pluralism and comes in consensual, agonistic and antagonistic forms.
- Both rest on the same insight: a single method can miss aspects of a phenomenon that another reveals — but they operate at different scales.

# What to Read Next

- [Qualitative vs Quantitative Research](qualitative-vs-quantitative-research.qmd) — the two approaches mixed methods combines.
- [Conducting Research Interviews](conducting-research-interviews.qmd) and [Case Study Research Design](../research-design/case-study-research-design.qmd) — methods that frequently appear in mixed designs.

# Further Reading

- Johnson, R. B., & Onwuegbuzie, A. J. (2004). "Mixed Methods Research: A Research Paradigm Whose Time Has Come." *Educational Researcher*, 33(7), 14–26.
- Creswell, J. W., & Plano Clark, V. L. (2017). *Designing and Conducting Mixed Methods Research* (3rd ed.). SAGE.
